items.q: Are monopolies always bad for consumers?
items.correct: Standard theory says monopolies restrict output and raise prices, but possible benefits include economies of scale, R&D investment and dynamic efficiency.
items.misconception: Monopolies are illegal and always harmful.
items.random_wrong: A monopoly is when one country dominates world trade.
items.why: Monopolies aren't automatically illegal — natural monopolies in utilities can be efficient. The analysis requires weighing static inefficiency against potential dynamic gains. Students treat the question as already settled.

items.correct: A cost imposed on a third party not involved in the transaction — e.g. pollution from a factory affects neighbours who didn't choose to bear it.
items.misconception: A negative externality is something bad that happens to a business.
items.random_wrong: A negative externality is a tax on a business.
items.why: The 'externality' is external to the market participants — borne by THIRD parties. Students miss the structure: producer, consumer, then someone else who didn't agree to be affected.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: Differentiated products and many firms describe:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Monopolistic competition. “Perfect competition” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Price equals marginal cost. “Price equals average cost” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
